questions.question: Explain the concept of locality of reference and its two types. Why is it important for cache memory?
keyPoints: Locality of reference is the tendency of a processor to access the same memory locations repeatedly
Two types: Temporal locality (recently accessed items accessed again soon) and Spatial locality (items near recently accessed items are likely to be accessed)
Important for cache because cache works on the principle that recently/frequently accessed data will be accessed again
Without locality, cache would be ineffective as every access would require main memory lookup

questions.question: Differentiate between write-through and write-back cache policies. Mention their advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Write-through: writes data to both cache and main memory simultaneously
Write-back: writes data to cache first, main memory updated only when block is replaced
Write-through advantages: data consistency, simpler implementation; disadvantage: slower writes
Write-back advantages: faster writes; disadvantage: potential data inconsistency, needs dirty bit

questions.question: Calculate the average memory access time for a system with L1 cache having hit time of 1 cycle, miss rate of 5%, and main memory access time of 100 cycles. What is the effective CPI if the base CPI is 1.0 and 30% of instructions memory access?
keyPoints: AMAT = Hit time + Miss rate × Miss penalty = 1 + 0.05 × 100 = 6 cycles
Memory stall cycles = Miss rate × Miss penalty × Memory accesses = 0.05 × 100 × 0.30 = 1.5 cycles
Effective CPI = Base CPI + Memory stall cycles = 1.0 + 1.5 = 2.5
Alternatively, average memory access = 6 cycles for memory operations only

questions.question: Explain direct mapping cache organization. What is the formula for determining cache line index? Why might direct mapping cause thrashing?
keyPoints: Direct mapping maps each memory block to exactly one cache line using: Line Index = (Block Address) mod (Number of Cache Lines)
Formula: Cache Line = Address mod NumberOfLines
Thrashing occurs when multiple frequently accessed blocks map to the same cache line, causing constant evictions and misses
Example: if blocks 0, 4, 8 all map to line 0 in a 4-line cache, alternating access causes cache to constantly reload

questions.question: What are the three main types of cache mapping techniques? Compare them in terms of hardware complexity and flexibility.
keyPoints: Three types: Direct mapping, Fully associative mapping, Set-associative mapping
Direct mapping: simple hardware (one comparator), inflexible, may cause conflicts
Fully associative: complex hardware (n comparators for n lines), most flexible, any block in any line
Set-associative: balanced hardware (set comparators), moderate flexibility, reduces conflicts by allowing multiple lines per set

questions.question: A computer system has a 16KB direct-mapped L1 cache with 64-byte blocks. Show how a 32-bit memory address is divided into tag, index, and offset fields. Calculate the number of cache lines, tag bits, and index bits.
keyPoints: Block size = 64 bytes → Offset bits = log2(64) = 6 bits
Cache size = 16KB = 16384 bytes, Lines = 16384/64 = 256 lines
Index bits = log2(256) = 8 bits
Tag bits = 32 - 8 - 6 = 18 bits
Address format: Tag(18) | Index(8) | Offset(6)
Verification: 18 + 8 + 6 = 32 bits

questions.question: Explain the concept of cache coherency problem in multiprocessor systems. Discuss the MESI protocol and its four states.
keyPoints: Cache coherency: ensures multiple caches have consistent views of the same memory location
Problem: when multiple processors have copies of the same memory block, writes by one processor should be visible to others
MESI Protocol states: Modified (exclusive, dirty), Exclusive (clean, only in this cache), Shared (clean, may be in other caches), Invalid (not present or stale)
Transitions: Write to Shared → write to Modified (obtain ownership), Read to Modified → write back and change to Shared, etc.
Ensures data consistency without excessive memory traffic

questions.question: A system has a two-level cache hierarchy. L1 cache has hit time of 1ns, hit rate of 95%. L2 cache has hit time of 10ns and hit rate of 90% (of remaining misses). Main memory has access time of 200ns. Calculate the average memory access time (AMAT).
keyPoints: Level 1 hit: 95% access in 1ns
Level 1 miss: 5% go to L2
L2 hit: 90% of 5% = 4.5% access in (1 + 10)ns = 11ns (includes L1 + L2 time)
L2 miss: 10% of 5% = 0.5% go to main memory, access in (1 + 10 + 200)ns = 211ns
Alternative method: AMAT = L1 hit time + L1 miss rate × [L2 hit time + L2 miss rate × Memory access time]

questions.question: Compare L1, L2, and L3 caches in terms of size, speed, and proximity to CPU. Explain why modern processors use multi-level cache hierarchy.
keyPoints: L1: smallest (16-64KB), fastest (1-2ns), closest to CPU, often split into data and instruction caches
L2: larger (256KB-1MB), slower (3-10ns), on or near processor chip
L3: largest (several MB to tens of MB), slowest (10-20ns), shared among cores
Multi-level hierarchy reason: trade-off between speed, size, and cost
L1 for most frequently accessed data, L2 for less frequent, L3 as last stop before RAM
Inclusive vs Exclusive: Inclusive (L3 contains L2 contains L1), Exclusive (only one level has data)
